name: Pasteurized Akkermansia MucT reduced weight regain after diet-induced weight loss
summary: The latest Akkermansia MucT human RCT supports metabolic biomarker relevance but is not a live-microbe or aging-function result.
statement: In adults with overweight or obesity after diet-induced weight loss, pasteurized Akkermansia muciniphila MucT led to less weight regain than placebo during 24 weeks of maintenance, with no serious treatment-related adverse events reported.
population_or_model: Adults with overweight or obesity after at least 8% diet-induced weight loss
time_horizon: 24-week maintenance phase after an 8-week low-energy diet
quantitative_note: Weight regain was 1.2 +/- 0.7 kg with MucT versus 3.2 +/- 0.4 kg with placebo (P = 0.012); net weight loss from baseline to end of maintenance favored MucT by 3.1 +/- 0.7 kg (P = 0.009).
caveats: The tested product was pasteurized rather than live.
Body-weight maintenance is aging-relevant metabolic context, not direct healthspan, frailty, or mortality evidence.
The trial was short relative to aging outcomes.
The source reports company and patent conflicts involving Akkermansia commercialization.
